appeal_letter:
Dear UnitedHealthcare Appeals Department,

I am appealing the denial of coverage for a bariatric surgery revision.

The denial states that, under UnitedHealthcare's Medical Management Guidelines, the request is not medically necessary because the clinical information provided does not demonstrate that maximum medical management (such as high-dose proton pump inhibitors and lifestyle modifications) has been attempted and failed for at least six months, and it characterizes surgical revision as a first-line treatment for reflux.

The basis of this appeal is that the single criterion the denial relied on, the six-month trial of maximum medical management, supports the requested revision when applied to the documented facts, and at minimum warrants an individualized exception where that threshold is not literally met.

The purpose of a conservative-therapy trial is to determine whether non-surgical management can resolve the condition before surgery is considered. According to the clinical record, the patient had a gastric sleeve five years ago and is now experiencing severe GERD that has caused esophageal strictures and Barrett's esophagus, which the record describes as pre-cancerous changes. The treating surgeon documents that the reflux is driven by an anatomical issue causing bile reflux, and that proton pump inhibitors will not correct that anatomy, making conservative therapy ineffective and, in the surgeon's assessment, dangerous in this case. On these facts the revision is not being requested as a first-line treatment for ordinary reflux; it is directed at an anatomical cause that a medication trial cannot address.

Where the documented cause of the condition is anatomical and the criterion's required therapy is medication that cannot reach that cause, a rigid six-month medication trial does not serve the criterion's clinical purpose and supports either application of the criterion in the member's favor or an individualized exception. The presence of Barrett's esophagus and esophageal strictures, as documented, further bears on the clinical risk of continued conservative management.

I request that the denial be overturned and that the bariatric surgery revision be approved as medically necessary. If the requirement is maintained, I request a written explanation of how the six-month maximum-medical-management criterion is to be satisfied given the documented anatomical cause, and identification of any additional documentation needed for an individualized medical-necessity determination.

I also request written disclosure of the applicable appeal rights, deadlines, and levels of review available for this denial, which were not stated in the denial letter provided.

Not legal or medical advice. Draft assistance only.

Respectfully,
Member
missing_evidence_checklist: Surgeon's signed clinical note or letter documenting the anatomical/bile-reflux cause and the opinion that PPIs cannot correct it
Endoscopy/pathology reports confirming Barrett's esophagus and esophageal strictures
Documentation of any proton pump inhibitor use to date, including doses, dates, and clinical response
Record of any lifestyle modifications attempted and their duration and outcome
Operative report and records from the original gastric sleeve procedure
Any prior authorization request and the specific Medical Management Guideline criteria cited by UnitedHealthcare
The denial letter's statement of appeal rights, deadlines, and levels of review, if issued separately
